data.title: SEALSQ: A Hold Despite Post-Quantum Ambition
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I do not think SEALSQ Corp. (NASDAQ:LAES) is an easy stock to underwrite, but it is also becoming increasingly hard to ignore.

The company works across secure silicon, device identity, and post-quantum cryptography, three areas that are quietly becoming non-optional across IoT, infrastructure, and industrial systems. Over the past year, it has also raised a significant amount of capital while expanding its technical footprint, and now, it looks like the company’s strategy is built around a post-quantum security roadmap.

To be clear, SEALSQ is not a mature security platform, and it is not a conventional semiconductor compounder either. We can see it with the fundamentals: small revenue and hugely negative margins, and much of its long-term narrative sits way, way ahead of the numbers. Even so, I think that the company’s setup going into 2026 looks more concrete than it did a year ago across its balance sheet, product development, and capital deployment, and I’m recommending a Hold on the stock.

What Does the Company Do?

I’d describe SEALSQ as a company that is trying to secure connected devices starting from the hardware layer, then extend that trust upward across the device lifecycle.

The core of the business today is still secure hardware. It designs and sells secure elements and secure microcontrollers that act as a hardware root of trust inside connected devices. These chips store cryptographic keys, handle authentication, and protect sensitive operations from tampering. This is where the bulk of current revenue comes from, and it is the entry point into most customer relationships.

On top of the silicon layer, we have provisioning and Root of Trust infrastructure. SEALSQ supports key injection and certificate provisioning at multiple stages, including during chip manufacturing, device assembly, and in-field deployment. This part of its business is important because large IoT and infrastructure deployments are more about how to manage identity and trust at scale.

The third layer is public key infrastructure (PKI) and trust services. This includes certificate issuance, lifecycle management, and authentication services that allow devices to be securely identified and managed over time. If you look at what the company has been up to recently, it’s clear that this is where it wants to move the business. In practice, this part of the model is still small in reported revenue, and I view it as an option on future mix shift, not a current earnings driver.

Beyond the core stack, SEALSQ is also expanding into custom ASIC and SoC design through IC’ALPS. It bought the French company last August, and the acquisition allows it to work with customers on tailored secure silicon rather than only standard products. It also pushes SEALSQ further upstream in the design process, and this can deepen customer relationships. At the same time, it brings some more complexity into the equation, especially around execution.

SEALSQ’s Growth Drivers, Positioning, and Market Opportunity

Across these markets, SEALSQ has relationships and design wins with Toshiba and Vodafone (smart metering), Legic (secure access systems), and other customers in the electronic lock manufacturing and medical industries in Japan, Taiwan, and the US. I’m reading this as a sign that the company is operating in real production environments, even though its revenue is still highly concentrated and customer order timing is another concern. Still, the takeaway from these opportunities is that SEALSQ is competing in environments where certification and compliance are part of the product.

Look at it this way: once any of the company’s secure elements or Root of Trust is certified and integrated into a customer’s design, it is very difficult for them to switch their supplier without going through a new requalification, regulatory review, and system redesign process. That creates a natural friction that benefits a business like SEALSQ, so it has the integration advantage.

For me, the clearest way to understand the company’s new positioning is to look at what it is trying to replace. It is trying to collapse that stack into a single security-first platform, starting at the hardware layer and extending through provisioning and trust services. Secure chips, key injection, certificate lifecycle management, and post-quantum readiness should work as one system rather than as loosely connected parts.

I think the post-quantum angle is a good example of where this distinction is even more crucial. Today, SEALSQ already supports post-quantum algorithms in its PKI services. On the hardware side, post-quantum secure microcontrollers are still largely on the roadmap, and the company expects to see some commercial impact a bit later than one would ideally like. That’s a gap between the hardware and the software side of things, and we will see more strides here over the next few years.

The most interesting part of how the company is planning for the future is its acquisitions, especially the planned Quobly one. Last week, SEALSQ announced that it has signed a non-binding MoU to make a minority investment into Quobly SAS, a French quantum computing company. Under the terms of that MoU, it could spend up to $200 million to acquire a majority stake of the company, albeit I’m not sure how much of the company they’re looking at buying out. Still, I take the announcement and potential move as a strategic signal. Quobly is a leader in its industry in France and Western Europe, so joining SEALSQ gives the latter an immediate complementary boost to its existing business and products. It also helps with its credibility.

Besides that, it means that we have to watch out for execution because investing alongside the quantum ecosystem requires you to have a plan for translating company insights and research into defensible, saleable products. Then, there’s the customization side of things. I mentioned its IC’ALPS acquisition last year, and that integration allows SEALSQ to engage its customers at the ASIC and SoC design level. That moves it upstream, where it can get the higher-value, longer-cycle projects.

Now, the market for these products and platforms is pretty large and growing quickly. TPMs (trusted platform modules) have an expected market value of nearly $5.5 billion in 2030, up from $3.3 billion in 2025. With post-quantum cryptography, the market size was $1.15 billion in 2024, and it has a CAGR of 37.6%, starting from 2025 and ending in 2030. For ASIC specifically, SEALSQ is also targeting a ¬$37 billion market. Those are just a few of the market opportunities.

These are eye-catching figures, and capturing even a tiny fraction of the markets should see SEALSQ become a company with annual revenues close to hundreds of millions by the end of those periods. But it’s not a straightforward trajectory, and I don’t want to equate the market size with inevitability.

For one, a $200 million pipeline isn’t a revenue backlog, but it’s a good starting point. At the same time, recording new design interest or wins doesn’t guarantee production revenue. I’m pointing this out because the investing market seems to have an idea around revenue figures and expectations for the next few years that is very different from the actual booked business the company has in the bag. In my view, the growth path that management seems to be going for rests on 3 key levers, which I’ll lay out below.

First, volume recovery in secure chips. The company has been managing a fairly weak period because of customer normalization and transition, but management expects baseline demand to recover. I think this is the least exciting driver, but it is the one that supports near-term revenue stability.

Second, there are new post-quantum product introductions, like chips that have FIP 140-3 and CC EAL5+ certification. We could start to see differentiation showing up materially here, but it is also where the execution risk is highest. Timelines matter, and customers will not redesign platforms lightly.

Finally, services attach over time. Provisioning and PKI services are supposed to ride along with hardware deployments. Today, we’re not seeing this as the case because they’re showing up only faintly in the numbers. I believe that long-term, it is the difference between a hardware supplier and a platform.

Overall, I think the outlook is very positive, and the question is whether SEALSQ can convert this early positioning into a sustainable revenue mix before dilution and competition start eating away the upside.

Where the Business Stands Today: The Numbers

SEALSQ’s financial profile only makes sense if you look at it across cycles, so here’s how I’d describe the last 2 fiscal years.

Period

Revenue ($m)

FY2023

~30

FY2024

~11

FY2025 (prelim)

~18

Q4 FY2025

~8

FY2024 was a reset year. Revenue fell to about $11 million, down sharply from FY2023, mostly because customers normalized their inventory and the company moved through a transition in its secure silicon roadmap. I’d say that the revenue decline exposed the operating leverage in the model and pushed the margins lower at exactly the wrong time.

Conversely, FY2025 was a rebound year. Going off what is in management’s preliminary results that came out a few days ago, we can see that revenue recovered to roughly $18 million, with a clear inflection in the second half of the year. Q4 alone contributed about $8 million, or close to 45% of full-year revenue, which tells me customer programs restarted and shipments resumed at scale. However, we must remember that this is still a sub-$20 million revenue business, and its revenue mix hasn’t changed much yet.

It’s the same picture with the gross margin. That was in the high-40s range in FY23, and it dropped to the mid-30s in FY24. It’s around the same range in FY25, albeit a tad higher as revenue rose, but still, growth without margin recovery does not fix the model. SEALSQ’s operating costs are even higher than ever because the components (SG&A, R&D, and sales) are built for a much larger business than it currently has, so it needs to scale revenue quickly.

With the balance sheet, it’s a nicer story. At the end of FY25, the company had around $425 million in cash, so it is exceptionally well capitalized for the medium term. That funding is mostly from equity offerings and warrant exercises, which is unsurprising, but it also changes the risk profile because liquidity isn’t a problem. The thing to consider is that it is shareholder dilution that is funding this growth, and we must consider whether the capital raised will produce enough incremental value per share to justify the company’s current valuation. That leads me nicely to discussing my position on the stock’s value.

I Believe That the Risk-Reward Favors a Hold

Today, SEALSQ’s stock is trading at $4.5 per share, and it has around 185 million shares outstanding. That gives it a market cap of $833 million. That valuation needs to be judged against where the business is today, even though the future looks promising.

The company exited FY25 with $18 million in revenue, and the valuation already embeds an incredibly generous forward view in my opinion. The company’s sales multiples are already at extremely elevated levels, and the only way they make sense is if it can record exponential sales growth while operating a more sustainable business model.

Now, let us look at the guidance for FY26. Management is guiding 50-100% revenue growth for the year, and that increase will largely come from the 3 levers I laid out in the previous section. That gives us a reasonable framework for expectations. Using FY25’s numbers as the anchor, SEALSQ should make between $27 and $36 million in revenue in FY26.

At today’s equity value of $833 million, the implied forward multiples look like this:

Scenario

FY2026 Revenue

Implied P/S

Low case

~$27m

~31x

Mid-point

~$31.5m

~26.4x

High case

~$36m

~23x

Those are not conservative multiples—they’re 8 to 10 times higher than the sector median of 3.5x. I don’t think they’re justifiable, especially for a business that is still reliant on hardware for sales while losing a ton of money and trying to transition into services as the main revenue driver. Simply put, even if we assume the absolute highest case within reason, so up to $40 million in FY26, the multiple still comes out to >20x. SEALSQ is still priced like it is a premium growth platform, and it is not that yet. There’s very little margin for error here.

If we also assume that it will recognize the rest of the pipeline in revenue (¬$160 million) across 2027 and 2028, it is still likely that there will be bigger share dilutions to fund that growth too, so it's not a straightforward ramp-up in share quality.

I can’t deny that the business has real momentum and improving fundamentals, but the fact is that we’re still waiting to see its products and trust services translate into revenue and materially improve gross margin. The latter, especially, is an outcome that is not fully certain heading into FY26, and I’m unwilling to pay such a premium for the stock at this time.

Key Risks to Look Out for and the Near-Term Catalysts

I’ve discussed several of the risks around SEALSQ’s business at several points during this article, so I'll just list a few below.

  • Revenue quality risk

  • Margin risk

  • Dilution and capital allocation

That last point is especially interesting because even though the company won’t struggle for cash in the medium term, I think it is likely that management will come back to the market for more funding at some point in the next two years. Why? Well, there’s the potential investment in and acquisition of Quobly ($200 million, and then some), and I believe that it is reasonable to assume that they will continue to pursue expansion and strategic investments in that timeframe. $400 million just won’t be enough.

Now, let me outline what I’m watching as catalysts moving forward. The most important is evidence that the second-half FY2025 inflection carries into FY2026. I think I speak for a section of the market when I say that in SEALSQ’s case, quarterly revenue consistency will start to matter more than headline annual growth over the next few years. A few strong quarters in a row will go a long way toward validating management’s outlook.

Another one is clearer disclosure around services and recurring components. I’d like to see some growth, however modest, in its deferred revenue or contract liabilities section on the balance sheet. In my opinion, that would signal that the business model is evolving in the right direction.

It’s a Hold for Me

I firmly believe that SEALSQ is operating in the right markets and with the right long-term questions in mind. The pieces are in place for it to grow quickly, and I’m watching how it executes. However, I’m not convinced that the current valuation represents a good entry point or has meaningful upside for now because it is far ahead of the company’s near-term trajectory.

I’d like to see broader revenue growth and recovering margins before getting more constructive again. If those pieces fall into place and the company outperforms the expectations it has set for itself, then I’ll be more inclined to consider changing my position. For now, it is a Hold for me.

data.title: BPRE: Interesting CRE CEF After The Conversion
data.description:
Thesis

Finance folks seem to love their rocks—after BlackRock and Blackstone, we are now going to examine a fund from Bluerock, an institutional alternative asset manager based in New York.

As per their webpage, Bluerock:

Is a leading institutional alternative asset manager based in New York with regional offices across the U.S. Bluerock has more than $19.5 billion in acquired and managed assets and offers a complementary suite of public and private investment programs, with both short and long-term goals, to individual investors seeking solutions aimed at providing predictable income, capital growth, and tax benefits.

In today's article, we are going to focus on the Bluerock Private Real Estate Fund (BPRE), a fund of funds in the CRE space. BPRE has been recently listed on the New York Stock Exchange as a CEF, thus now falls in the respective market segment.

What is the origin of this fund?

BPRE is interesting because it used to be an interval fund until late 2025, when it got listed:

New York, NY (September 25, 2025) – Bluerock is pleased to announce shareholder approval of a key proposal in connection with the listing of the Bluerock Total Income+ Real Estate Fund (“TI+” or the “Fund”) on the New York Stock Exchange. The proposal¹, recommended by independent proxy advisory firms Institutional Shareholder Services Inc. and Glass Lewis & Co. and the Fund’s Board of Trustees, was overwhelmingly approved by shareholders at the Fund’s special meeting held on September 25th, with over 81% of votes cast in favor of the initiative. In an earlier special meeting held on September 3rd, shareholders approved 13 additional proposals related to the listing.

Remember that interval funds are vehicles that are not listed; liquidity is not readily available to purchase or sell shares:

An interval fund is a type of investment company that periodically offers to repurchase its shares from shareholders. Their shares typically do not trade on the secondary market. Instead, their shares are subject to periodic repurchase offers by the fund at a price based on net asset value. They are permitted to (and many interval funds do) continuously offer their shares at a price based on the fund’s net asset value.

Due to this structure, many investors are locked in for long periods of time and thus do not benefit from the liquidity offered by listed vehicles. Many interval funds structure themselves this way because they purchase illiquid assets and thus need to structure their cash holdings for redemptions accordingly.

Once an interval fund gets listed, the market assigns a 'mark-to-market' to the underlying holdings, thus discounts to NAV appear. The CEF now trades with a -35% discount after its listing in December 2025.

What does the fund hold?

Unlike many CRE funds we reviewed, BPRE is a 'fund of funds', meaning it does not hold CRE loans or buildings outright but holds shares in other private CRE funds:

If we look at the holdings list, we can see the name holds shares in other private CRE funds, such as the CBRE Logistics Partners, the Carlyle Property Investors fund, or the Bain Capital Real Estate Fund I. These are not vehicles that can be accessed by retail investors because they are private and have very high minimum investment amounts. Thus, holding BPRE gives a retail investor access to the underlying investment vehicles that would otherwise be unreachable.

BPRE's holdings are diversified when it comes to the targeted segments:

  • Industrial funds: 28.3%.
  • Multisector funds: 20.5%.
  • Specialty: 14%.
  • Residential: 8.4%.
  • RE debt: 19.3%.
  • Cash: 9.6%.

The holdings list provided by the fund does contain some details regarding the funds (such as start date, average LTVs, etc.), but it is by no means exhaustive or very transparent. Unlike outright CRE REITs, where an investor has transparency into every loan, one buys into a portfolio of other funds here.

Does a large discount to NAV mean a massive upside?

The quick answer is no. The reality is more nuanced, however. We would split the large discount into two segments:

  • Actual write-downs are priced by the market.
  • Discounts that will close eventually.

The issue with many private interval funds and related accounting is that they do not need to take write-downs until loans mature, and they cannot be restructured anymore. In other words, the accounting is very permissive in terms of kicking the can down the road. This simply means the fund does its accounting at full notional value, when in reality, a true mark to market would be much lower. But this is a book value accounting versus a mark-to-market one.

When you allow a vehicle to be priced on an exchange, the market will do the pricing for you and assign your holdings a certain value. Right now, the market is telling us there are write-downs to be taken in the holdings (by each underlying fund), and there is also a discount to account for the uncertainty associated with other holdings.

This is not unusual. Keep in mind the trading levels for CRE REITs:

Ares Commercial Real Estate Corporation (ACRE) trades with a 49% discount to book, while KKR Real Estate Finance Trust Inc. (KREF) trades with a 42% discount. We wrote research articles for these funds and articulated why there are substantial write-downs to be taken from a fundamental perspective, with the market pricing that in.

So what does BPRE offer over CRE REITs?

BPRE is a bit of a different animal when compared to a CRE REIT. The CEF does not own CRE loans or buildings but shares in a very diverse portfolio of private CRE funds. The name is thus very, very diversified when it comes to managers and geographic reach. We like this aspect.

On the other hand, an investor here has very little transparency in the actual risks, but it is more of a faith-based diversified approach to CRE investing.

The fund currently comes with the following analytics:

  • AUM: $3.47 billion.
  • Yield: 8.5%.
  • Payment period: monthly.
  • Management fee: 1.5%.

The name recently transitioned to monthly payment dates in order to make itself more appealing to investors.

We find BPRE to be an interesting diversification proposal in the space, with the caveats around a lack of transparency. Given the recent listing, it is hard to tell how much of the discount represents what we would call 'repressed liquidity', i.e., investors who wanted to get out before but could not. We will follow BPRE going forward and are a 'Hold' for now.

Conclusion

BPRE is a CRE closed-end fund. The name is a new addition to the liquid space, having been an interval fund until December 2025. BPRE is a fund of funds, rather than holding CRE loans outright. The name provides access to a portfolio of private CRE funds from very reputable managers. Despite its large discount to NAV, the market is signaling write-downs that have not been taken yet by the underlying holdings, very similar to the listed CRE REIT space. We are watching this name closely, but we are on hold for now.

data.title: Thornburg Strategic Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Despite volatility, our focus continues to be on generating attractive income and risk-adjusted total returns over the long term.

Portfolio Managers

Lon Erickson, CFA

Portfolio Manager

Ali Hassan, CFA, FRM

Portfolio Manager

Christian Hoffmann, CFA

Head of Fixed Income

Supported by the entire Thornburg investment team

Market Review

U.S. fixed income markets concluded 2025 on a positive note, with broad gains driven by Federal Reserve rate cuts, cooling inflation, and broadly resilient economic data despite challenges like the U.S. government shutdown and labor market softness. The Federal Reserve continued its easing cycle with 50 basis points of rate cuts, including a widely expected 25bp cut at the December FOMC meeting that lowered the Fed Funds rate to 3.50-3.75% alongside a $40B/month Treasury bill purchase program, though the FOMC minutes reflected continued divisions within the committee. November saw significant volatility in rate expectations with December futures probabilities dropping below 30% at one point following hawkish signals from Chair Powell and other Fed officials. Inflation showed continued disinflation with November CPI coming in below consensus at 2.7% year-over-year (core 2.6%), while mortgage rates dropped for three straight weeks to end 2025 at their lowest point for the year. The government shutdown delayed most employment and inflation data in October with impacts continuing into November, creating what analysts called a "data vacuum."

Treasury yields fluctuated throughout the period, with short-end yields declining approximately 20-30 basis points as rate-cut expectations increased. The curve continued to steepen with the 30-year yield rising as short-end rates fell. The job market in late 2025 was characterized by labor market softening, with unemployment reaching a four-year high of 4.6% in November amid layoff announcements from major companies including Meta, Target, Amazon, Molson Coors, and Applied Materials.

Fourth Quarter 2025 Performance Highlights

In 4Q25 the portfolio (I share class) (TSIIX) returned 1.19%, -1 basis point behind the Bloomberg U.S. Universal Index. Year to date, the portfolio (I share class) returned 7.56%, -2 basis points behind the index.

Sector allocation and security selection contributed positively to relative performance with the portfolio’s CMO and corporate allocations providing uplift of 5 basis points each. The portfolio’s overweights to BB and Not Rated issues resulted in a net positive contribution of 15 basis points to relative performance as credit spreads modestly narrowed.

The portfolio maintained an average duration of 4.17 versus 5.79 for the index. Duration and yield curve positioning contributed a combined 15 basis points to relative performance for the quarter. The yield curve continued to steepen across the quarter with short-end rates falling and long-end rates rising driven by a combination of Fed cuts and favorable economic data.

Current Positioning and Outlook

The portfolio remained positioned with a longer duration and higher quality bias compared to its long-term range, driven by attractive real yields and tight credit spreads. Although we maintain a generally cautious view of the data center and AI sectors, many hyperscaler firms demonstrate strong credit profiles and will require ongoing investment to expand their AI infrastructure. In recent months, we have identified attractive opportunities within project finance, specifically through lending secured by data centers under construction with leases to investment-grade tenants. These transactions are not intended as broad sector calls but rather as select opportunities characterized by high barriers to entry, which can deliver favorable valuations, robust structures, and desirable tenant profiles.

The global economy has shown surprising resilience despite mounting pressures from the delayed impact of tariffs and constrained fiscal flexibility. With ongoing uncertainty, careful portfolio construction remains essential. Instead of crowding into popular trades or implementing static allocations, we urge investors to focus on strategies that balance liquidity, potential returns, and diversification, while staying flexible to adapt to new opportunities. In 2026, those who favor high-quality fixed income, selectively add real assets for stability, and seek undervalued equity sectors may benefit most.

We maintained broad diversification across fixed-income sectors with an emphasis on sectors demonstrating resilience or offering compelling risk/reward profiles identified through our research. Credit spreads remain tight, particularly within private market areas that have experienced sharp growth. Despite a generally strong background for equities, shares of several large alternatives firms have seen declines. More broadly, recent bankruptcies and instances of fraud may be indicative of light covenants typically seen late-cycle. Investors should consider if their holdings justify the risks of credit, illiquidity, falling yields, and limited transparency.

Basis Point (bp) – A unit equal to 1/100th of 1%. 1% = 100 basis points (bps).

U.S. Treasury securities, such as bills, notes and bonds, are negotiable debt obligations of the U.S. government. These debt obligations are backed by the "full faith and credit" of the government and issued at various schedules and maturities. Income from Treasury securities is exempt from state and local, but not federal, taxes.

A bond credit rating assesses the financial ability of a debt issuer to make timely payments of principal and interest. Ratings of AAA (the highest), AA, A, and BBB are investment-grade quality. Ratings of BB, B, CCC, CC, C and D (the lowest) are considered below investment grade, speculative grade, or junk bonds.

Credit Spread/Quality Spread - The difference between the yields of securities with different credit qualities.

Duration - A bond's sensitivity to interest rates. Bonds with longer durations experience greater price volatility than bonds with shorter durations.

Yield Curve - A line that plots the interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality, but differing maturity dates.

Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) - The branch of the Federal Reserve Board that determines the direction of monetary policy. The FOMC is composed of the board of governors, which has seven members, and five reserve bank presidents. The president of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York serves continuously, while the presidents of the other reserve banks rotate their service of one-year terms.

Fed Funds Rate - The interest rate at which a depository institution lends immediately available funds (balances at the Federal Reserve) to another depository institution overnight.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligation (CMO) - A type of mortgage-backed security that creates separate pools of pass-through rates for different classes of bondholders with varying maturities, called tranches. The repayments from the pool of pass-through securities are used to retire the bonds in the order specified by the bonds' prospectus.

Mortgage Pass-Through - A security consisting of a pool of residential mortgage loans. Payments of principal, interest and prepayments are "passed through" to investors each month.

U.S. Emerging Markets Index, and the non-ERISA eligible portion of the CMBS Index. The index covers USD denominated, taxable bonds that are rated either investment-grade or below investment-grade.

data.title: Macquarie Corporate Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index credit spreads widened 4 basis points (bps) over the quarter, underperforming duration-matched Treasurys by 5 basis points but still generating a +0.84% total return. Shorter-duration, wider-trading sectors such as autos and office real estate investment trusts (REITs) led performance. Airlines also outperformed amid strong results from United Airlines (not held in the Fund) and an upgrade by Moody’s to Baa3.

The cable and media sectors led underperformers over the quarter as they remained under pressure from secular weakness and mergers and acquisitions. The technology sector also lagged amid heavy supply to fund data center build outs and growing capital expenditure (capex) needs. Investment-grade supply for the quarter totaled $311 billion (source: Bank of America), the strongest fourth quarter supply in history as hyperscaler and M&A issuance contributed to the record total. This included an $11 billion deal from Verizon Communications, Inc. (VZ) to fund the acquisition of Frontier Communications, Inc. (FYBR) and artificial intelligence (AI)-related deals from Alphabet, Inc. (GOOGL) ($17.5 billion), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) ($15 billion), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) ($30 billion). Technicals remain strong and demand for investment-grade credit continues to be robust with more than $20 billion of inflows to investment-grade funds for the quarter and $69 billion for the full year (source: Lipper).

Source: Bloomberg, unless otherwise noted.

Within the Fund

For 4Q, Nomura Corporate Bond Fund Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund’s benchmark, the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index.

Key contributors to performance:

  • Non-Cyclicals – Security selection contributed to performance, driven by an underweight to longer-dated, higher-quality sectors such as pharmaceuticals and healthcare which lagged amid a steepening Treasury curve and improved risk sentiment.
  • Banking – Security selection generated positive performance, driven by an overweight to hybrid debt as these shorter-duration (to call) and higher-beta issues outperformed over the period.
  • High Yield – Exposure to this out-of-benchmark asset class generated positive performance as high yield outperformed investment grade given its shorter-duration profile and positive risk technicals.

Key detractors from performance:

  • Electric – Security selection generated negative performance, driven by an overweight to long-end spread duration within utilities as longer-dated issues lagged amid the rise in long-end rates.
  • Energy – Similarly within the independent exploration and production (E&P) sector, an overweight to longer-duration holdings was a drag on performance amid rising rates over the period.
  • Futures – Net long Treasury futures positions to hedge curve exposure detracted from performance as rates rose over the last two months of the quarter.

Outlook

We believe the strong technical backdrop, stable credit fundamentals, and a relatively benign interest rate environment will remain supportive for credit spreads in 2026 with a somewhat stable macroeconomic backdrop as many of the themes from 2025 (rate volatility, tariff impacts, and so forth) carry over into 2026. Risks will be more idiosyncratic in nature and skewed towards individual credits and sectors in the form of M&A, leveraged buyouts, and a significant increase in AI capex. We believe these factors will create dislocations in the market and will provide opportunities for investors. As a result, we expect spreads to trade in a narrow range of 75 bps to 95 bps for 2026 as investors will demand additional premiums to absorb the uptick in gross and net issuance, with relative value relationships getting repriced across sectors and ratings. We favor BBB-rated credits as earnings have been relatively strong and technicals are favorable with the BBB index share now at a decade low (source: Barclays). A key risk to our view is growth significantly slowing down as higher inflation further pressures consumers with tighter lending conditions.

Gross corporate bond issuance is expected to reach an all-time high of $1.8 trillion (source: JP Morgan), driven by three main factors: a wave of refinancing as more than $1 trillion of maturities come due; an uptick in M&A transactions; and a dramatic increase in debt-funded capex from the technology sector. The AI investment boom is front and center, with hyperscalers (Alphabet, Amazon, Microsoft Corp., Oracle Corp., and Meta) projected to account for more than $300 billion in new supply (source: JP Morgan) as data center construction and infrastructure accelerates. Net supply (gross supply less redemption activity including maturities, calls, tenders, and coupon income) is also expected to increase in 2026 at roughly $302 billion (source: JP Morgan) but still remaining manageable and well below the peak experienced during the pandemic. Given that spreads are expected to remain at historically tight levels, the sheer volume of supply, especially from technology and media, will be a key driver of sector performance, highlighting the importance of sector selection. Financials and healthcare should outperform based on strong fundamentals, manageable event risk, and lower supply pressure, while technology, media, and retail are likely to lag as elevated issuance and leverage weigh on valuations. Non-cyclicals should outperform cyclicals, reflecting a more cautious consumer outlook and subdued retail demand. We expect that finance companies, mainly business development companies (BDCs), will continue to be volatile with fears growing around the lack of transparency into the private credit assets held by BDCs. This could also impact life insurers given the rising use of private credit in general account portfolios. We believe such volatility will create investment opportunities in the sector. Finally, demand from institutional and foreign investors should remain robust based on historically attractive all-in yields and with investor positioning remaining manageable.

data.title: Root And Branch: A Case Study In Assessing Portfolio Biodiversity Risk
data.description:
By Sara Rosner | David Hutchins, FIA | Henry Smith, CFA

Biodiversity risk is more nuanced and complex than many investors think. For instance, there’s a widespread belief that deforestation poses the biggest nature-related risk for most portfolios. But when we applied our proprietary biodiversity risk-assessment framework for a client, we found that water risk—not deforestation—was the portfolio’s biggest exposure. Our analysis of the MSCI AWCI Index using the same framework shows water risk is elevated for many companies, highlighting just how important it is to assess biodiversity risk accurately.

16%

of the MSCI ACWI is exposed to the highest level of biodiversity risk

35%

the share of MSCI ACWI for which water risk is high or very high

14%

the share of MSCI ACWI for which deforestation risk is high or very high

The importance of biodiversity as a nature-related risk in investors’ portfolios has become better understood in the past few years. Investors are beginning to appreciate how complex and nuanced biodiversity risk can be. For example, there’s a widespread assumption that deforestation is the biggest biodiversity risk in many portfolios. That’s understandable, given media attention around the topic, and because large-scale deforestation contributes to climate change, which can have a material impact on investments across the globe.

But such assumptions can fall short of reality. We recently analyzed the biodiversity risks in the equity allocations of a large UK pensions provider1 and found that water stress, not deforestation, was the biggest risk (Display).

Case Study: A Deep Dive into Portfolio Biodiversity Risk

Percentage of Portfolio at High or Very High Risk from Water and Deforestation Impact

This may seem surprising, given that the impacts of water stress tend to be localized and not so obviously global as those of deforestation and its links to climate.

But this particular client is not an outlier. Our analysis of the ENCORE2 industry biodiversity-risk database shows that the proportion of the MSCI ACWI Index for which water-related risk is high or very high is 35%—not very different from the client’s 31%. (The index also has a bigger exposure to high or very high deforestation risk—about 14%.) The insight underscores how important it is for investors and investment managers to analyze and identify their nature-related exposures correctly.

To that end, we have developed a proprietary risk-assessment framework that can identify nature-risk exposures across portfolios at the sub-industry level. In turn, this enables analysts to assess and engage with the potentially high-risk companies identified. We believe the framework may help reduce portfolio risk and unlock better returns.

Mapping Portfolio Biodiversity Risks

According to the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures, nature-related risks are potential threats to an organization arising from dependency and impact on nature—the organization’s own and those of society too. Dependency and impact are, respectively, physical and transition risks.

For example, fisheries that rely on water quality to sustain their fish stocks have a high dependency on nature, as they are exposed to the physical risk that water quality might deteriorate. Mining companies and property developers have a high impact on nature; as laws, regulations and trade practices change (transition) to reflect society’s growing awareness of biodiversity risk, they may become exposed to legal, reputational, market and other risks.

Using dependency, impact and related risks as a framework, we draw on the ENCORE database to map biodiversity risk exposure at the GICS3 subindustries level (Display).

Biodiversity Risk Matrix Identifies Highest-Risk Industries and Companies

AB’s Biodiversity Risk Matrix: Industries by Impact and Dependency

The industries that fall in the upper right quadrant have both high dependency and impact and, therefore, the most nature-related risk. Our analysis shows that 15.7% of MSCI ACWI falls into this quadrant. For the equity portfolio in our client case study, the proportion is significantly lower, at nearly 10% (Display).

Case Study: What the Matrix Tells Us About a Portfolio’s Biodiversity Exposures

Client Portfolio Mapped by AB’s Biodiversity Risk Matrix (Percent)

Understanding how dependency and impact risks are distributed across industries in a portfolio is a vital first step, but it’s also important to dive deeper to understand the risks at the issuer level. The ENCORE database lists risks individually and classifies them according to whether they are dependencies or impacts. Using this information, we can apply the framework to identify specific risks across a portfolio. This was how we discovered the extent to which the case-study portfolio’s biodiversity risks were dominated by water-related exposures (Display).

Case Study: Client Portfolio’s Biodiversity Risk Exposures

Most of these exposures fell in the dependencies category, but two of the top five impact exposures were also water-related—area of freshwater use and emissions of toxic soil and water pollutants. This level of information is potentially helpful in deciding where to focus efforts to mitigate the portfolio’s biodiversity risks.

Having established which industries in a portfolio have the highest nature risk, we can identify individual holdings within those industries that we may wish to target for further research to understand which biodiversity risks are most material for them.

Company Engagements: Consistency and Comparability

For active portfolios managed by AB, this level of research may also lead us to engage4 directly with issuers to better understand their exposures and, if appropriate, work with them to help mitigate risks. We decide which issuers to engage with on a case-by-case basis, according to whether engagement is in clients’ best interests. When undertaking an engagement for clients whose strategy is implemented mostly with third-party funds, we partner with appointed managers to ensure that biodiversity is a priority for them. The engagement process may require multiple meetings over time to monitor issuers’ progress in achieving their risk-reduction goals.

We have engaged on biodiversity risk with issuers in various client portfolios. These issuers include, for example, Linde (LIN), a UK-based supplier of industrial gases; US water-treatment company Ecolab (ECL); and Swiss processed foods group Nestlé (NSRGF)(NSRGY)(Display).

Identifying Companies with High or Very High Impacts and Dependencies

All fall in the top-right quadrant of the risk matrix. Linde ranks high for impact and moderate for dependency; Ecolab also ranks high for impact but higher than Linde for dependency. Nestlé ranks highest of the three on both counts. Below, we use Linde and Ecolab to illustrate how we engage on water-related risks and Nestlé on deforestation-related risks.

The process begins with defining the objective of the engagement. In the case of Linde, we wanted to know the water-risk implications of its hydrogen production—especially its move to producing green hydrogen. Traditional hydrogen production uses steam methane reforming, in which steam reacts with methane to produce hydrogen. Green hydrogen, which is powered by renewables, uses an electric current to split water into hydrogen and oxygen. Both processes are water-intensive.

When engaging with Linde, we noted that it had reported considerable water savings, but its steam consumption had increased. The company explained that this had been possible because of a move to closed-loop production processes in which steam is fed back into the production cycle. It planned to use a similar technique to conserve water when producing green hydrogen. We asked Linde to consider introducing a company-wide water-usage target, and it said it planned to do so soon.

With Ecolab, we wanted an update on its effort to help customers conserve water and how it might be affected by plans to service water-hungry AI data centers. While the company had made considerable progress with conservation (226 billion gallons out of a 300 billion target), it noted that further gains were being complicated by increased water demand. But the expansion of data centers and utilities to power them was also creating opportunities for new efficiencies in energy and water supply, and the company was looking for commercial water-solution opportunities across the AI value chain.

Our engagement with Nestlé on deforestation illustrates some of the challenges of risk mitigation and the value of monitoring progress. In 2010, the company aimed to eliminate deforestation from five key supply chains—palm oil, soy, meat, paper and sugar—by 2022. It progressed well except for palm oil, which is sourced from high-risk countries: deforestation in the chain had fallen only 70% by 2020. We tracked progress, and in 2021 the company, aided by satellite and field monitoring, had eliminated deforestation across 90% of the chain. Its next goal was to be deforestation-free in all supply chains by the end of 2025.

Not Just Risks, but Opportunities, Too

Biodiversity exposures are material risks for many portfolios and need to be addressed appropriately, in our view. Our framework—supported by specialist, third-party industry data—can show how these exposures are distributed across portfolios and identify issuers with high nature-related risks. Combined with fundamental research and, where appropriate, issuer engagement and stewardship, it can lead to a deep understanding of issuer- and portfolio-level risks and the actions needed to mitigate them.

But it’s not just about risk: it’s about opportunity, too. For example, our engagement with Linde improved our understanding of how green hydrogen—an important step in the move to a low-carbon economy—could be produced in a way that conserves water usage. And Ecolab helped us understand how the growth in AI data centers might lead to enhanced water and energy efficiencies and related commercial opportunities.

As part of a broad-based active investment strategy, the framework, in our view, can not only identify and mitigate nature-related risks in portfolios, but it may help unlock better returns, too.

[1] The analysis applied the framework to the client firm’s default investment offering for UK defined contribution (DC) pension savers to illustrate how the framework can surface biodiversity-related risks and highlight potential areas for deeper review. The results are provided for illustrative purposes only and are not intended as investment advice.

[2] The Exploring Natural Capital Opportunities, Risks and Exposure (ENCORE) database is maintained by Global Canopy, the UN Environment Programme Finance Initiative and UN Environment Programme World Conservation Monitoring Centre.

[3] Global Industry Classification Standard. Among the Standard’s various categories, the category “subindustries” provides the most granular classification of industries.

[4] AB engages issuers where it believes the engagement is in the best interest of its clients.

The authors would like to thank Max Lulavy, Responsible Investing Research Analyst at AB, for his contributions to this article.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

References to specific securities discussed are for illustrative purposes only and should not to be considered recommendations by AllianceBernstein L.P. It should not be assumed that investments in the securities mentioned have necessarily been or will necessarily be profitable.

The "target date" shown in a fund's name refers to the approximate year when a pension scheme member expects to retire and begin withdrawing from his or her account. Target date funds gradually adjust their asset allocation, lowering risk as a member nears retirement. Investments in target date funds are not guaranteed against loss of principal at any time, and account values can be more or less than the original amount invested—including at the time of the fund’s target date. Also, investing in target date funds does not guarantee sufficient income in retirement.

data.title: Ultragenyx: From Bone To Brain, Capitulation Creates A Free Option On The Neuro Pipeline
data.description:
EQUITY RESEARCH | BIOTECHNOLOGY

Company: Ultragenyx (RARE)

Rating: BUY

Price Target: $55.00

Current Price: $22.75

Implied Upside: +142%

_____________________________________________________

Executive Summary: Capitulation Creates Asymmetry

Ultragenyx's 45% dip after the recent failure of the Phase 3 trial in osteogenesis imperfecta, OI, has created a unique contrarian opportunity, where the stock is trading below our valuation of its revenue-stage franchise, while offering upside as other late-stage product candidates, for example, GTX-102 in Angelman's syndrome (Phase 3 data expected later this year with a high probability of success).

I. The Commercial Safety Net

Ultragenyx has several commercial stage products.

The commercial franchise includes Crysvita (Burosumab), the first and only FDA-approved treatment for X-linked hypophosphatemia (XLH), which affects 1 in 20,000 people. For the last 12 months ending Q3 2025, the company has $391 million in Crysvita revenue, growing 14% annually in the nine months ending Q3 2025 (seven years after launch). Dojolvi (Triheptanoin) is gaining momentum in treating long-chain fatty acid oxidation disorders. Mepsevii (vestronidase alfa) is the first FDA approved treatment for Mucopolysaccharidosis VII (MPS VII).

The company provided preliminary revenue guidance for 2025 of $672 million to $674 million (higher than the consensus of $651 million), representing 20% annual revenue growth compared to 2024.

II. The Crown Jewel: Angelman Syndrome (GTX-102)

The company's GTX-102 (Apazunersen) in Angelman's syndrome could be one of the most undervalued assets in rare diseases. This genetic disorder causes severe developmental delays, intellectual disability, and disorders of speech, balance, and movement. It affects one in 15,000 live births or 500,000 people worldwide. The disease is treated symptomatically and with therapy and has no disease-modifying treatments.

Phase 1/2 trials showed significant benefit, like improvements in cognition, motor skills, and behavior, suggesting disease modification, which earned Breakthrough Therapy and Fast Track designation from the FDA. Compared to Ionis' (IONS) ION582, GTX-102 has better data and has a time lead.

Data from the Phase 3 ASPIRE trial in Angelman's syndrome is expected in the second half of this year. Our probabilistic modeling shows a 62% probability of the trial meeting its primary endpoint.

III. The Silent Winners: GSDIa and Sanfilippo Syndrome Type A

The metabolic components of the developmental stage pipeline add further upside.

GSDIa is a severe genetic disorder that causes an inability to regulate blood sugar, and causes hypoglycemia. It affects 1 in 100,000 live births worldwide and has no FDA approved therapies. Ultragenyx's DTX401 is a AAV8-based gene therapy that showed a significant reduction in cornstarch use (to treat hypoglycemia) and improved quality of life in pivotal trials, earning Fast Track designation. Compared to Moderna's (MRNA) mRNA-3745, it has the advantage of one time treatment (avoiding chronic dosing) and has a time lead. The company completed rolling BLA submission in December 2025 and I expect FDA approval in the second half of the year. We estimate an 85% probability of FDA approval in the first attempt at PDUFA.

Sanfilippo Syndrome Type A is a severe genetic disorder that prevents the body from breaking down a complex sugar molecule called heparan sulfate, causing toxic buildup in various organs, including the brain. It is estimated to occur in 1 in 70,000 births worldwide and has no FDA approved therapies. Pivotal studies for the company's gene therapy UX111 showed significant improvement in cognitive function and reduction in heparan sulfate. Ultragenyx has a significant first mover advantage compared to competitors like Denali Therapeutics (DNLI) (in Phase 1/2 study). The FDA issued a complete response letter [CRL] for the approval application in July 2025 due to specific chemistry, manufacturing, and controls, CMC related observations, without any questions on efficacy or safety. The company plans to resubmit the Biologic License Application [BLA] this year. We assign a 75% probability of FDA approval.

IV. Financial Analysis & Discounted Cash Flow Valuation

Ultragenyx ended Q3 2025 with ~$425M in cash. The operating cash use was $445M in the last 12 months; however, with the termination of the OI program, we expect the 2026 cash burn to decrease to <$80M/quarter, extending the cash runway past the Phase 3 Angelman's syndrome readout in the second half of the year. Long-term debt is ~$763M.

We assign a $55 price target based on a Sum-of-the-Parts (SOTP) analysis after removing the OI program from the analysis.

Asset / Segment Methodology Value Contribution ($) Per Share Value ($)
Commercial Base (Crysvita/Dojolvi) DCF (11% WACC, 2% terminal) $2,100M $26.00
GTX-102 (Angelman) rNPV (60% POS, Launch '27) $1,300M $16.00
DTX401 (GSDIa) rNPV (85% POS, Launch '26) $650M $8.00
UX111 (MPS IIIA) rNPV (75% POS, Launch '27) $400M $5.00

TOTAL $4,450M $55.00

V. Risks to the Bull Thesis

Risks to the bull thesis include a decline or flatlining of the Crysvita franchise, an unexpected CRL for planned regulatory submissions for UX111 or DTX401, or increasing competition to the Angelman's syndrome franchise from competitors like Ionis.

VI. Final Recommendation

We are buyers of Ultragenyx stock in this panic. The sell-off after the failure of the OI pipeline has created an opportunity where investors are rating the stock below the value of the commercial franchise, with no value assigned to the late-stage developmental stage pipeline.

Recommendation: BUY

Price Target: $55

data.title: Voya MidCap Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Actively managed mid-cap growth strategy that relies on fundamental research and analysis to identify companies exhibiting superior capital investment and core profitability with attractive risk-reward profiles.

Portfolio review

U.S. equity markets closed 4Q25 on a positive note, driven by moderating inflation and robust earnings. The S&P 500 Index advanced 2.66%, and the technology-heavy Nasdaq Composite gained 2.57%. The healthcare and communications services sectors led, while real estate and utilities sectors lagged. Large cap stocks beat small cap stocks, and value outperformed growth stocks.

The U.S. Federal Reserve supported markets in 4Q25 with two 25 basis point rate cuts. At its December meeting, the Fed signaled a balanced tone and improved growth and inflation outlook. At the same time, AI remained a dominant theme, with strong headlines around innovation and elevated capital investment by major technology firms. Increased spending on AI infrastructure and adoption trends reinforced optimism about its role as a key driver of future growth.

For the quarter, the Fund underperformed the Index on a NAV basis due largely to unfavorable stock selection. Stock selection in the consumer discretionary, industrials, and health care sectors detracted from results. Conversely, stock selection within the energy, consumer staples, and information technology sectors contributed the most to performance.

Key detractors from performance included Roblox Corp., Doximity, Inc., and Natera, Inc.

An overweight position in Roblox Corp. (RBLX) detracted from performance. The stock declined following quarterly earnings, which signaled slower profit growth and uncertainty around future bookings.

An overweight position in Doximity, Inc. (DOCS) detracted from performance. The stock declined following a quarterly earnings report signaling cautious guidance and uncertainty after recent healthcare policy changes.

Not owning Natera, Inc. (NTRA) detracted from performance. The stock rose after its earnings announcement, signaling a strong revenue beat.

Key contributors to performance included Vera Therapeutics, Inc., Comfort Systems USA, Inc., and Dollar Tree, Inc.

Owning a non-benchmark position in Vera Therapeutics, Inc. (VERA) contributed to performance driven by positive phase three clinical trial results for their kidney disease treatment and AI-enabled drug discovery.

An overweight position in Comfort Systems USA, Inc. (FIX) contributed to performance. The stock rallied after the company reported stronger-than-expected 3Q25 revenue, driven by robust demand for data centers and AI-related infrastructure.

A non-benchmark position in Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR) contributed to performance driven by better-than-expected third-quarter earnings due to increased seasonal demand. The company's move to offer prices above $1 boosted margins and profitability.

Current strategy and outlook

The U.S. economy enters 2026 with a foundation of resilience. We expect moderate growth supported by consumer spending and productivity gains, helped by Voya MidCap Opportunities Fund lower rates. The Fed's recent rate cut reflects heightened concern over labor market softening and confidence that tariff-driven inflation pressures will fade.

While the outlook suggests a soft landing with moderate growth and gradual disinflation, caution is warranted as geopolitical risks and policy uncertainty persist. In addition, broadening of the narrow market leadership beyond mega-cap growth, underpinned by AI innovation and corporate investment should support market growth.

data.title: TAL Education: Bullish Short-Term And Long-Term Outlook
data.description:
I'm still rating TAL Education Group (TAL) as 'Buy'.

My earlier October 16, 2025, write-up predicted a Q2 outperformance. I was right with its Jul-Aug '25 top-line and EPS surpassing consensus by +4% and +49%, respectively, as per S&P Capital IQ.

In my view, investors are likely to be pleasantly surprised by TAL's upcoming quarterly disclosures too.

My Bet is on Q3 Beat

The group's Sep-Nov 2025 results will be released next Thursday on Jan 29.

I believe that TAL will outdo the analysts' expectations.

The market sees its turnover going up +26.3% year-on-year to $0.77B in 3QFY26. During the same period, the company's consensus bottom line is $0.08/share, implying a +26.6% expansion.

There are a number of positive signs for TAL, which I've identified below.

The "Monthly Active Users/MAUs" for its K12 tutoring platform "Xueersi.com" grew +37% and +39% in October and November '25, respectively. These were obtained from Chinese consultancy QuestMobile, whose data isn't publicly accessible. The same metrics for its peer New Oriental (EDU) were inferior at -9%/+7% for Oct/Nov.

At the 2QFY26 briefing on Oct 30 last year, TAL revealed that "our deferred revenue balance was USD 822.7 million." This translates to a +60% YoY jump. The figure is also 7% higher than the sell-side's forecasted third-quarter topline detailed above.

EDU participated in Goldman Sachs' (GS) Nov 24, 2025 investor event. Management's guidance is for its "non-GAAP operating profit margin to expand" going forward with the ongoing execution of "cost control tasks." The information is taken from GS's research note (unavailable in public) titled "Internet Trip Takeaways" published on the same day.

TAL's G&A-to-revenue contracted by -3.5ppts (percentage points) to 2QFY2026's 14% on YoY terms. Note that "G&A" refers to "General & Administrative."

My take is that the education-focused enterprises in China are pivoting to an emphasis on profits. This supports my assumption of TAL's EBIT margin improving by +500 bps (basis points) year-on-year in the 3rd quarter. The consensus estimate is +390 bps. I've taken into account EDU's bullish outlook and TAL's Q2 cost reduction.

I project that sales and earnings for TAL will both increase by more than +30% in 3QFY26. Its shares will most probably do well after reporting better-than-anticipated numbers.

Eyes on Industry Tailwinds

TAL should be a beneficiary of the Chinese education sector's excellent prospects in the long run.

UBS Group (UBS) recently issued a publication with the title "China Online Entertainment And Education Marketing Deck" on January 9, 2025.

The "After School Tutoring/AST" business in TAL's home market is supply-constrained. UBS's research indicates that there's a "potential supply shortage of 40-60M (Chinese) students," as "the government remains committed to regulating" this segment. It's reasonable to think that sub-scale and non-listed players are finding it hard to get policymakers' go-ahead to expand in the industry.

The firm noted at the Q2 call its "small class enrichment programs" experienced "higher enrollments" with "the continued expansion of our offline learning center network." It's clear to me that the group has been ramping up capacity. That favors TAL gaining share in an undersupplied environment.

Separately, there's growing interest in AI-enabled learning, which the company can capitalize on.

In the preceding section, I cited statistics from QuestMobile. According to the same source, its AI-related educational app "Xiaosi" witnessed a +150% YoY MAU surge in the final month of 3QFY26.

TAL's late-Oct '25 briefing commentary highlighted the roll-out of a "tutoring AI companion for our learning devices" branded as "AI Think 101" in mid-2025. That product subsequently secured "the industry's highest rating (Level 4)" from "the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology."

Imagine you are a parent choosing tablets for your kids. Those highly rated by reputable industry bodies will be the top choices.

The consensus's FY25-28 revenue and earnings CAGR projections for TAL are impressive at +26% and +49% (source: S&P Capital IQ), respectively. I view these figures as realistic after assessing the industry's dynamics.

You Need to Consider These Variant Views

If the regulators lower the entry barriers in China's AST market, the competitive pressures for TAL might intensify.

The actual AI-driven learning outcomes may fall short of expectations. That scenario could lead to a drop in the sales of its related services and tools.

The Final Word

I've stayed as a bull on the stock. Its future for the short- and long-term looks promising.

My mid-Oct '25 piece outlined a "target (Enterprise Value-to-Sales) of 1.5 times" benchmarked with "its three-year historical mean." I'm sticking with that.

Its listed comparable EDU's EV/S is less demanding at 0.93x, which is justified by its relatively weaker MAU numbers.

TAL's multiple re-rating upside is +36% (1.5/1.1), given its current EV-to-sales of 1.10 times (source: S&P Capital IQ).

data.title: Netflix: Hold The Stock After The Earnings Results
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) is one of the best-known entertainment service providers globally, operating in as many as 190 countries. The company started out by offering TV series, documentaries, and feature films, but quickly grew into the games scene as well.

Over the past 5 years, NFLX's stock price saw quite a bit of volatility. In the first half of 2022, Netflix lost roughly 80% of its market value. The turnaround in the sentiment came quickly, however, and the stock price kept gradually increasing until mid-2025, significantly outperforming the broader market (SPY). In the past quarters, sentiment once again turned negative, and the share price cratered.

As NFLX recently reported its quarterly earnings results, the aim of this article today is to give a balanced view, whether it is worth investing in NFLX's stock now or not. My arguments are going to be revolving around the macroeconomic environment, and around company-specific metrics, including growth, profitability, and valuation.

Financials

Revenue

Netflix beat analyst estimates on both the top and bottom lines in the most recent quarter. Revenues came in at $12.05B, $80 million above analyst estimates, representing a growth of 17.6% year-over-year. The drivers of the revenue growth were membership growth, higher pricing, and increased ad revenue. The firm achieved higher revenue growth than anticipated, primarily due to the better-than-expected membership growth and ad revenue growth, partially offset by an unfavorable FX environment. To put membership growth into perspective, let us look at some numbers. Netflix now 325M million subscriptions - equivalent to about 1 billion viewers. These viewers are also watching more content, as the number of hours watched is up by 2% year-over-year in the second half of 2025. Lastly, Netflix's original content is gaining more and more traction. Viewing of Netflix originals was up 9% YoY. Non-branded hours, however, slightly declined as a result of the WGA strike in 2023-2024.

In my view, these figures are excellent, in light of the current macroeconomic environment. Netflix is essentially selling entertainment products, which are considered to be discretionary and nonessential. During times of low consumer confidence, when people are less certain about their financial outlook, they normally cut back on discretionary spending. Despite consumer sentiment still being just above the 10-year lows, people do not seem to be cutting on their Netflix subscriptions. Actually, exactly the opposite is happening; membership is growing faster than anticipated. Maybe Netflix is the budget entertainment option, as people do not want to spend larger sums by going out?

Let us look at the revenue growth breakdown regionally. I find it appealing that all of the regions are exhibiting double-digit growth. Growth accelerated significantly in the U.S. and Canada, reaching 18% YoY, while growth in the EMEA remained stable at 18%, but showed a slight decline on an FX-neutral basis. These two regions remain the largest by revenue. LATAM growth also accelerated, reaching 15%, while the APAC growth significantly slowed, but still remained in the higher teens.

Looking forward, Netflix expects revenue growth to be between 12% and 14% in 2026. This is somewhat lower than what they achieved in the previous years, and probably the primary reason why the stock price fell right after the earnings release. Q1 guidance also came in below expectations.

Does it justify the sell-off, though? In my view, no. The firm is having a large number of initiatives to keep up the growth. They are planning to expand their live events offering with events like the World Baseball Classic in Japan; they plan to expand into video podcasts, and also expand their gaming business. At the same time, ad revenue in 2026 is expected to double. Creating more high quality original content to strengthen the core business and the acquisition of Warner Bros. should also boost the firm's financial performance in the coming year.

Profitability

It is not only the revenue growth that impresses, but also Netflix's profit margins. If we look at the past three years, we can see that NFLX managed to improve its profitability metrics significantly.

Over the past year alone, operating margin expanded by 3 points. Looking forward, in Q1 the company expects the operating margin to be 32.1%, while for the whole year they anticipate 31.5%.

All in all, I find Netflix's business attractive from a growth and business fundamental perspective.

Valuation

To assess whether NFLX is a good buy at the current price levels, let us look at a set of traditional price multiples. The following table compares NFLX's metrics with those of the communication services sector median, as well as with its own historic averages.

We can clearly see that NFLX's stock is trading at a significant premium compared to the sector as a whole, but at a substantial discount to its historic averages.

Let us look a bit deeper into this. If we compare NFLX's current metrics to its past metrics, I believe that the discount is justified. While growth remains strong, the growth is slowing. Normally, slowing growth leads to multiple compressions; therefore, it is understandable why the stock is cheaper now than it was in past years.

The comparison to the sector as a whole, however, may be somewhat misleading, as it contains a large number of firms, which may have entirely different business models from NFLX's. So let us narrow down the peer group to the movies and entertainment industry only. The following table contains valuation, profitability, and growth metrics. Undoubtedly the firm's profitability and growth metrics are appealing, with one of the strongest net profit margins and one of the strongest EPS FWD growth metrics in the industry. The valuation, however, appears stretched, especially looking at the P/CF and P/S metrics.

While I believe a certain premium is justified, I think that the stock is still somewhat expensive, especially considering the negative sentiment around the firm and the recent downward momentum. I would like to see the stock price fall another 10% to 20%, before I would consider it a buy from a valuation point of view.

That being said, I would also like to point out that the firm is returning significant value to its shareholders through share buybacks. During the quarter, they bought back 18.9M shares for $2.1B, and another $8 billion remains from their buyback program. While it is a small amount compared to their market cap, it might be an indication that management believes that the shares are attractively valued.

Risks

Let me highlight a few risks, which I believe are key when considering an investment in Netflix's business.

1. Competition. Netflix themselves said that they are not necessarily competing against other firms in the industry, but competing against all activities that people do. I completely agree with this statement.

Consumer preferences evolve quickly. I believe one has to provide quality content continuously to remain competitive and keep the attention of people. As discussed earlier in the article, I am also not quite sure, if people would be so engaged with Netflix, if they were financially healthier. Would they prefer going out, attending concerts, or going on excursions, instead of watching content on Netflix?

2. Content Netflix does create a large number of original content, but their success is mainly driven by a handful of shows that became fan favorites, including Stranger Things. Not all of their movies and series are able to gain so much traction and attention. How often are they able to create such viral content, and how long can they keep running certain fan-favorite shows before they become boring? Again, consumer preferences evolve quickly, and if they are not able to keep up, their growth might slow significantly.

Conclusions

Netflix beat analyst estimates on the top and bottom lines, despite slowing growth figures. Revenue kept growing across all regions, driven by membership growth and ad revenue growth, while profitability improved. Double-digit growth is still expected for the 2026 full year.

From a valuation point of view, I am more cautious about the firm. I would like to see the share price fall 10% to 20% before I would consider the stock a value play.

Risks include competition and changing preferences of the consumer.

Overall, I rate NFLX as a hold.

data.title: Microbot Medical: High-Risk High-Return Stock, Still Hard To Value
data.description:
Microbot Medical (MBOT) is a robotic medical tech (MedTech) company I have been observing for a while. It developed the world’s first single-use remotely-operated robotic tech, LIBERTY, that allows the streamlining of endovascular interventions that enable minimally invasive procedures and reduced radiation exposure to clinicians. Its business model is B2B, where it primarily sells its solution to hospitals and clinics aiming to reduce high CAPEX associated with traditional surgical robots.

Share performance has been volatile since it went public in 1992, with the stock trading below its historical highs for the most part. Nonetheless, despite being down -1.42% YTD, the stock has seen some tailwinds as of late, with 14.3% upside when we zoom out and view it from a 1-year return standpoint. It currently trades around $2 per share.

I initiate my coverage with a neutral rating. My 1-year price target of $1.9 suggests that MBOT is fully valued.

Financial Reviews

As in the case of any company developing novel technologies, MBOT has largely been a pre-revenue company with zero revenues. Nonetheless, the growth outlook appears compelling.

In Q3 2025, MBOT seems to have entered the first step into revenue generation given the FDA 510(k) clearance of LIBERTY. With the Limited Market Release [LMR] taking place as of late, MBOT has effectively commenced its first commercial activity.

As of Q3 2025, MBOT was unprofitable, much due to the same reason I mentioned. In Q3 2025, net loss was $3.6 million, slightly similar to where it was the same time last year at $3.22 million. In my opinion, while net loss widened, MBOT appears to have done sufficiently well to maintain loss levels YoY, especially when the company today is at the stage where it is hard to judge based on profitability alone. Meanwhile, the increased spending for Q3 was also justified due to the company entering the first step of commercialization. In Q3, SG&A expenses represented the biggest drawback to the bottom line, possibly as MBOT ramped up commercial teams and 3PL logistics to support its upcoming nationwide sales efforts.

Overall, liquidity is decent. MBOT ended Q3 2025 with around $80 million of current assets, which includes cash and marketable securities.

Such a significant increase from 2024's $5 million range was much due to the common stock issuance done in 2025. MBOT seems to have reinvested the proceeds into marketable securities. With operating cash flow [OCF] being in the red historically, MBOT has been getting its liquidity from financing cash flows.

Catalyst

In my opinion, catalysts remain moderate to high, especially with MBOT sitting at the narrative transition between R&D and a commercial-driven growth story. As such, the main catalyst here is the transition from LMR to Full Market Release [FMR] in Q2 2026 at the Society of Interventional Radiology [SIR] conference:

The company is expected to initiate the limited market release of the LIBERTY System during the current fourth quarter of 2025 with the broader launch anticipated to occur in April 2026 during the Society of Interventional Radiology conference.

Source: Seeking Alpha

The launch during the conference also marks the company’s entrance into a broader sales effort nationwide to drive more than $4 million of projected revenue in FY 2026 as per analysts’ estimates.

The relatively growing market in the treatment of peripheral artery disease [PAD] in the US, which requires endovascular procedures, as per the research from Polaris, suggests that there is an attractive blue-ocean opportunity for a disposable single-use robotic tech.

Risk

Overall, I believe that MBOT is a high-risk, high-return, pre-revenue opportunity for investors. With most of the focus on Q2 2026 FMR, the biggest risk would be that of execution. Even if MBOT could demonstrate success with its pilot project with Emory Hospital, broader sales efforts of a novel technology such as MBOT’s robotic platform may involve potential drawbacks from the lack of market readiness to adopt the technology itself. Meanwhile, I also consider managing a larger volume of sales to be MBOT’s next test, especially with the system being single-use, requiring careful logistics planning to fulfill reordering.

Another thing I notice that could present a bottleneck for the stock to trend higher is the relatively high number of warrants due to the company raising sizable capital from common stock issuance in 2025. With the exercise price of the 2025 issued warrants ranging roughly between $1.5 and $2, I may expect MBOT to face selling pressure from the existing holders if share price increases, preferably from MBOT seeing a rating upgrade into 2026 upon the FMR at the SIR conference.

Valuation / Pricing

My target price for MBOT is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs. bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection, based on the consensus analysis:

  1. Bull scenario (50% probability) assumptions - MBOT’s LIBERTY sees rapid adoption across major hospital networks in the US upon the successful FMR in Q2 2026, disrupting the endovascular procedure industry with its single-use disposable platform. I estimate the number of total shares, including dilution, to be 68 million.

  2. Bear scenario (50% probability) assumptions - MBOT sees sluggish sales activities, primarily driven by supply chain issues related to reordering processes across hospitals. MBOT sees wider net losses as the expenses to build up a nationwide commercial sales team outweigh the revenue generation. I assume higher shares dilution to 90 million as MBOT raises more capital from common stock issuance.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $1.9 per share. At this point, I would conclude that it remains challenging to properly value MBOT as a pre-revenue company, as my projection yields a figure that suggests MBOT is fully-valued today. I give the stock a neutral rating.

Conclusion

MBOT is potentially transitioning into a commercial growth story in 2026. Given the FMR, there is enough catalyst for the stock for a re-rating. Execution remains important across sales as well as supply chain, considering the single-use robotic novel technology. The risk remains high, since expansion from a pilot into a broader nationwide sales entails a whole set of new challenges. While MBOT has enough liquidity to support all these, the capital raising last year has also resulted in a lot of outstanding warrants that could create selling pressures on MBOT. My analysis suggests that it remains tough to value MBOT. I rate the stock neutral.
